items.q: What does a gene code for?
items.correct: Typically a polypeptide (protein) — through transcription to mRNA and translation by ribosomes.
items.misconception: A gene codes for a specific physical feature like eye colour directly.
items.random_wrong: Each gene codes for an organ or body part as a whole.
items.why: Students learn 'gene for eye colour' shorthand, then think genes directly produce traits rather than producing proteins that contribute to traits.

items.q: What does insulin do to blood glucose?
items.correct: It lowers blood glucose by causing cells (especially liver and muscle) to take it up and store it as glycogen.
items.misconception: Insulin raises blood glucose to give the body energy.
items.random_wrong: Insulin destroys glucose in the bloodstream so it cannot be used.
items.why: Students associate sugar with energy and 'doing something with sugar' with raising energy, missing that insulin removes glucose from blood.

items.q: Can sucrose move in both directions in the phloem?
items.correct: Yes — translocation goes from any source (e.g. leaves) to any sink (e.g. roots, fruits), so direction varies seasonally and between phloem sieve tubes.
items.misconception: Phloem always carries sucrose downwards from leaves to roots.
items.random_wrong: Phloem carries water upwards and sucrose downwards in the same tube.
items.why: Students conflate phloem with xylem in terms of being unidirectional; they don't realise source-sink relationships can reverse (e.g. early spring, roots are sources).

items.q: Counter-current flow in fish gills maintains:
items.correct: A concentration gradient along the gill
items.why: The answer examiners want is: A concentration gradient along the gill. “A steady ATP supply” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
Correct answer: Trachea and spiracles. “Lungs” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
